segments.s283-g25: چو is a temporal conjunction meaning “when” introducing a subordinate clause. پیروز برگشت is a past-tense clause: برگشت is a 3rd-person past verb “returned,” and پیروز (here used as the person/title ‘the victor’) is the semantic subject; note the poetic/inverted word order where مرد (‘man/hero’) follows the verb as an appositive or emphasis on who returned. از نبرد is a prepositional phrase “from the battle” giving source.
segments.s283-g26: درفش دلفروز is a noun phrase: درفش ‘standard/banner’ followed by its proper name دلفروز. بر پای کرد is a verbal phrase where کرد is the 3rd-person past “did/made” and picks up its subject from the previous clause (the returning man); بر پای (literally “on/at the foot/base”) functions like the idiom برپا (to erect), so the whole means “set up/raised (the banner).”
segments.s283-g9: همی is a classical/perfective-aspect particle here giving an ongoing or emphatic past ('was raining' / 'kept raining'). تیر is the grammatical subject (arrow(s)) and بارید is the simple past verb 'rained/fell' used metaphorically. همچون تگرگ is a simile (lit. 'like hail') modifying the verb phrase.
segments.s283-g10: بر is the preposition 'on/upon'. آن is the demonstrative 'that' qualifying اسپر (an archaic noun for 'horse'); the following کرگ functions as a post-nominal name/epithet attached to اسپر (possessor or epithet follows possessed noun in older Persian). The second بر repeats the preposition for the coordinated nouns ترک and ترگ ('and upon X and upon Y').
segments.s283-g11: یکی here introduces a single item ('one' / 'a certain'). تیر گرگین is a nominal phrase 'the arrow of Gorgin' (گرگین is a proper name; post‑nominal genitive is common in epic style). بزد is the past tense 'struck' and بر سرش is the prepositional phrase 'upon his head' where -ش is the enclitic possessive 'his'.
segments.s283-g12: که introduces a relative clause modifying the preceding noun (the head / the thing struck). بردوخت is a past participial/past passive sense 'was sewn/embroidered'. با ترگ رومی is an instrumental phrase 'with a Roman scrap/patch (ترگ = rag/patch, رومی = Roman)'. برش here is compressed/poetic and functions to indicate the item related to the stitching (literally 'on it/him'); the exact lexical senses given in the list don't include this enclitic usage, so the precise dictionary senses supplied don't match.
segments.s283-g13: بلرزید is the past 'he/it trembled'. بر زین is the locative phrase 'on the saddle' (بر + noun). ز سختی uses the poetic preposition ز (= از) meaning 'from/because of' and سختی 'force/severity' — together 'from the force'. سوار is the subject 'the rider' (word order reflects epic flexibility: verb + locative + cause + subject).
segments.s283-g14: یکی تیر دیگر is 'one/another arrow' (یکی = 'one' used to present a single instance; تیر = 'arrow'; دیگر postpositively = 'other/another'). بزد is the past 'struck'. نامدار follows as an epithet 'the famed/renowned (one)' — here it identifies the person hit or the notable man affected by the arrow.
segments.s283-g15: هم آنگاه is an adverbial phrase 'at that same moment / immediately then'. ترک is the subject 'the Turk'. اندر آمد is a classical phrasing 'came in' used idiomatically here to mean 'fell/was thrown in (to a prone state)'. نگون is an adjective 'down‑faced/overthrown' describing his condition; the adjective after the verb indicates the resulting state.
segments.s283-g16: ز introduces source ('from') in poetic register. چشمش is 'his eye' (چشم + enclitic -ش = 'his'). برون آمد is the compound verb 'came out/forth'. از درد modifies the verb as cause ('because of the pain'), and خون is the thing that came out ('blood') — the sequence is literal 'from his eye out came, from pain, blood'.
segments.s283-g1: برون‌رفت is a past action (came out / emerged) placed before a sequence of names/epithets; the proper name گرگین follows and is modified by the epithets that follow it (نهم کینه‌خواه). کینه‌خواه here is a substantive adjective (an epithet) meaning 'seeking revenge' or 'avenger' describing the named man. Word order is typical of classical Persian: past verb + subject phrase with epithets.
segments.s283-g2: ابا اندریمان functions as a proper name (Aba-Andariman) following the previous named figure; the short preposition ز (here poetic) introduces origin 'from' and takes the genitive noun توران (Turan). سپاه is the head noun 'host/army' governed by ز. The whole phrase gives the origin/affiliation of Aba Andariman.
segments.s283-g3: This is a noun phrase: جهاندیده and کارکرده are adjectival epithets placed before the head noun دو مرد. جهاندیده is an adjective 'worldly‑wise' and کارکرده is a participial adjective 'having acted / experienced' (here 'battle‑hardened/experienced'). The order (epithets → numeral → noun) is normal in classical Persian.
segments.s283-g4: برفتند is a past plural verb 'they went / departed' matching the plural subject 'دو مرد'. The coordinating و links the two verbs/clauses. جستند here has the rarer sense 'to seek / to search' (not 'to leap'), so جستند جای نبرد means 'they sought the place of battle' (جای = 'place', نبرد = 'battle').
segments.s283-g5: به نیزه بگشتند is a compact classical clause: به + instrument/means + verb. Here به introduces the instrument/position 'with/to the spear(s)' and بگشتند is a past plural 'they turned' or 'they wheeled' (i.e. turned their formation/turned on their spears). The coordinating و links the next result clause; بشکست is a transitive past 'broke' and پست qualifies what was broken ('the lower / inferior ranks').
segments.s283-g6: This is a simple past clause: کمان برگرفتند 'they took up the bow(s)'. برگرفتند is the past plural 'they lifted / took up' and is followed by the object کمان. هر دو is an idiom ('each two' → 'both'), and به دست is an adverbial phrase meaning 'in hand'/'by hand' (به here marks manner; دست = 'hand').
segments.s283-g7: ببارید is an intransitive past 'it/they rained' used impersonally here: 'arrows rained'. تیر is the subject (arrows), and the agent/source is marked by از + کمان سران 'from the bows of the chiefs' (سران = 'leaders/chiefs'). This is a common epic image 'arrows rained from the leaders' bows.'
segments.s283-g8: This clause strings prepositional and verbal elements: به روی اندر آورده literally 'having brought upon the face' — به روی (on/upon the face/surface), اندر (in/onto) and آورده (past participle 'brought') form a participial phrase describing where/what was brought down. کرگ اسپران is a compound noun phrase following آورده (object or apposition); in context it names what was brought down upon them. Word order (prepositional phrase → participle → noun phrase) is typical of classical Persian poetic compression.
segments.s283-g17: فرود آمد is a past tense verbal phrase meaning 'descended/swooped down' (فرود + آمد). از باره گرگین is a prepositional phrase: از ('from') + باره ('occasion/charge/assault') + گرگین (a proper name, Gorgin) = 'from Gorgin's assault/charge'. چو گرد is a simile 'like a dust-whirl' (چو = 'like/as', گرد = 'dust/whirl'), modifying the manner of فرود آمد.
segments.s283-g18: سر اندریمان is the direct object (سر = 'head', اندریمان = proper name Andarimān). ز تن دور کرد is a causative-style construction: ز ('from') + تن ('body') + دور ('away/removed') + کرد ('did/made') = 'made (it) distant from the body', i.e. 'tore the head from the body.' Note ز is the poetic form of 'از'.
segments.s283-g19: به فتراک بربست is 'he fastened (بربست) the saddle-cloth (فتراک) onto (به) [the horse]'. The verb برنشست ('sat upon') follows, with خود ('himself') emphasising that he himself mounted. The two past verbs are consecutive actions.
segments.s283-g20: This short clause is elliptical and inverted: نوند سوار is a nominal phrase (نوند = a rare name/term; سوار = 'rider') and نبرده is a perfective negative 'had not taken/been taken'. به دست ('by hand' / 'into hand') functions adverbially = 'taken captive'. Together, the clause means 'no (such) rider had been taken (i.e. captured).' The subject/agent is omitted and understood from context.
segments.s283-g21: بر آن ('upon that') sets the temporal/causal frame. تند بالا برآمد is a verbal cluster: تند ('swiftly') + بالا برآمد ('rose up/arose'), with دمان ('hem/skirt') as the affected nominal that follows the verb. Classical Persian allows the verb to precede its noun (verbal fronting).
segments.s283-g22: This is a compact descriptive sequence naming the agent and the items/positions: همیدون (a proper name) followed by locative/benefactive prepositional phrases. به بازو = 'on (his) arm', به زه = 'to/on the bowstring', بر کمان = 'on the bow'; the verb is omitted (elliptical) and supplied from the previous action (he placed/bridled/held).
segments.s283-g23: به نیروی یزدان is an instrumental/ablative phrase 'by the power of God' (به used instrumentally). که او بد پناه is a relative clause modifying یزدان: که ('who/which') + او ('he/God') + بد پناه is an archaic phrase meaning 'was a refuge/protector' (pāʻh is 'refuge'); the verb is elliptical/understood. This is a typical lofty, compressed epic construction.
segments.s283-g24: به marks the beneficiary/recipient: 'to/for the ... king'. The sequence پیروز بخت جهاندار شاه is a stacked epithet in apposition: پیروز ('victorious'), بخت ('fortune/luck' used as an epithet here), جهاندار ('world-ruler/sovereign'), شاه ('king'). Together they form the elaborate title: 'the victorious-fortune, world-ruling king.'

sentences.s283-s13: When the victor returned from the battle, he set up the Dalfrouz standard.
sentences.s283-s5: Arrows rained like hail upon that horse and upon the Turk and upon the rag.
sentences.s283-s6: One arrow of Gorgin struck his head, which had been stitched with a Roman patch.
sentences.s283-s7: The rider trembled on the saddle from the force; another arrow struck the famed one.
sentences.s283-s8: At that very moment the Turk fell down, and blood came out of his eye from the pain.
sentences.s283-s1: Out came Gorgin, the vengeful one, and Aba Andariman of the Turanian host.
sentences.s283-s2: Two experienced men, worldly-wise and battle-seasoned, went forth and searched for the place of battle.
sentences.s283-s3: They wheeled with their spears and broke the lower ranks; both took up the bow in hand.
sentences.s283-s4: Arrows rained from the chiefs' bows, brought upon their faces the kerag of the horsemen.
sentences.s283-s9: He swooped down from Gorgin's onslaught like a whirlwind and tore Andariman's head from his body.
sentences.s283-s10: He fastened the saddle-cloth on and himself mounted; no rider had been taken captive.
sentences.s283-s11: At that the hem of his garment flew up swiftly; Hamidun set the bow on his arm and the bowstring upon the bow.
sentences.s283-s12: By the power of God, who was his refuge, to the victorious-fortune, world-ruling king.
